# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for our Python Flask API. The app needs to install dependencies from requirements.txt, expose port 5000, and run the Flask application. Keep it simple and straightforward for quick deployment to production.
# Category: container_security

# Start with a base image of python which has pip preinstalled
FROM python:3-slim

# Set working directory in Docker file 
WORKDIR /app

# Copy requirements to the docker container. The -f flag allows us not only find files but also directories inside them (recursively). Including . for all contents of current dir will include your pipfile and pyproject, if exists or any dependencies that it depends on — like virtualenvs with packages
COPY requirements*.txt ./
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt --no-cache-dir   # The : is not needed when we have already declared the working directory in previous line 'WORKDIR' above and hence '/app'. If you see an error, it means there are no files to copy which may happen if a file like .flaskenv does exist within your WORKING DIRECTORY.
# You should replace or add this when starting with command - ./<yourfile>:$LABEL  (example 'docker run myimage python app.py') but you've mentioned requirements and doesn’t contain a file, so I will keep it as is without any modification. It starts from the current directory for rest of commands in docker container .
COPY ./ /app/   # Copies all contents within folder to '/workspace'. You can replace './src with where your source files are located' (current dir). If you see an error, it means there is no file or directories that needs copy. The directory structure should be exactly the same as in requirements.txt ie flask app - src , templates and static folder etc..
EXPOSE 5000    # This port will expose when we run container from docker command (docker run <image_id>) . Flask by default listens on PORT='5001'. You can replace it if you want, here I use 'port:32768', but remember to have the same value in your flaskserver.py and Dockerfile where needed (Flask should look for this port). 
# Run command with FLASK_APP=app.py will run application inside container on that specific file as per .env environment variable used within docker-compose or individual script which is running python app in your particular situation/environment ie flaskserver.py if exists, else it may not work because the Python executable needs to be part of PATH for Flask (it's by default inside container).
ENTRYPOINT ["python", "flaskserver.py"]  # 'Flask should look in this file/environment variable when starting server from outside Docker'. If you see an error, it means there is no such setting to run application or that the python executable can't be found where Flask app lives (it must live inside container). Replace these with your own settings.
